title: Future Plan
html:
Her mother tilts her head across the table. "What is it, dear?"

I realize I have been watching her for longer than I intend to. There is a decision I have been weighing through most of the meal, and at this point the steak is finished and the weighing has not resolved itself, which means I should probably just say it.

"Mother." I set my fork down. "I went to the Heka temple this morning."

"The temple?" Her brow lifts. "What for, dear?"

"To test my mana aptitude. With the jewel."

Father looks up from his plate.

"Elena," he says carefully. "You already tested."

"I know. I wanted to check again." I keep my voice even. "The result was different. I have a talent in water magic. The priest recommended several books and I bought them on the way out."

The silence after that is the kind that happens when people are deciding whether what they heard is real. Father sets his cutlery down. Mother's hands press flat against the table.

"Elena, child," Father says. "Are you certain they weren't mistaken? The mana jewel has never—"

"Returned an incorrect result. I know." I reach back and draw Lily forward by the wrist. She steps up beside my chair without a word. "Lily came with me. She stood in the testing chamber."

Father looks at Lily. Lily meets his eyes and gives a small, precise nod.

Something shifts in his expression — not dramatically, just a release of tension in the jaw, like a door opening on a room he had stopped expecting to reach. Beside him, Mother's lips move in something too quiet to be words and her hands fold together in her lap.

I let the moment settle. "I would like to keep this quiet for now. No announcement, no letters to anyone."

"Of course," Father says immediately. "Of course, Elena."

He turns to the room and makes a brief gesture, taking in the old butler and the two household maids stationed near the sideboard. All three nod without needing to be told twice. The butler looks at his shoes for a moment, then looks very deliberately at the wall, and his jaw is doing something that suggests he has made a firm private decision about the wall.

I turn back to the table. My plate is empty, which is good timing.

Mother's expression for the rest of dinner is the one people wear when they have received news so good they are slightly afraid to examine it directly. Father is more composed, but he refills my water glass twice without being asked. For a family that has not produced a mage in 999 years, I suppose this is an evening that warrants it.

***

Upstairs, the window chair is where I left it. I settle into it and pull the Blue Mage Tower history from the stack on the small case, since it is the thinnest volume and I still have the evening.

The chapter I want is near the back: a section on the physical conditions for mana practice. The foundational temple texts covered this briefly; this volume is more specific. According to the tower's records, this world operates under 1 moon, 1 sun, and 4 fixed stars whose positions are used for navigation and calculating ambient mana density by region. The capital city holds among the highest ambient mana density in the empire, which is why the temple district and the tower buildings are concentrated here rather than elsewhere.

That is useful information. It means I am practicing in the best available environment through simple geography.

The mana intake method is described as a breathing exercise: breathe in and, as the air enters, direct attention inward toward the space where mana is stored, imagining the intake flowing upward into the mind and holding there. The text runs to three pages of supplementary guidance but that is the working summary.

I close the book, sit back, and try it.

The first few breaths produce nothing I can identify. I breathe slowly and direct my attention inward, imagining the incoming air separating into its ordinary component and something slightly different, something that routes toward the mind rather than the lungs. After maybe ten breaths, a faint pressure develops behind my eyes. Not painful, just present, the early stage of a headache that has not committed yet.

Something is moving in there, or the nearest thing to moving that a sensation can be without being physical. I sit with it until it stabilizes, then open my eyes.

Not impressive from the outside, probably. From the inside it is enough to work with.

The tier 1 and tier 2 sections are in the same chapter. Tier 1 is generating water: combining the mana in the body with the ambient mana in the environment to produce liquid from the combination. The text notes that without environmental mana even a large personal pool is insufficient, which limits what mages can do in mana-poor regions regardless of their own capacity. Tier 2 is control: shaping the generated water and learning to draw more efficiently on environmental mana to increase output volume.

As a mechanical description it reads like a combination of biology and physics, which are not the subjects I would have chosen to encounter in a world where horses are still the primary transport. I read both sections twice and set the book on the armrest.

There are no spell words in this system. No incantations, no required gestures. The magic is entirely intent and mana control, which means it is either simpler or harder than a spell-based system depending entirely on how well a person can hold and direct something they cannot see.

I close my eyes, locate the faint pressure from the earlier exercise, and try to pull a thread of it outward toward my hand.

For a moment nothing happens. Then something shifts at the surface, and I open my eyes.

Water forms just outside the window glass, roughly enough to fill a small teacup, and drops with a Splash! onto the stone sill below. I watch it run off the edge into the garden. The pressure behind my eyes sharpens briefly and fades.

'Faster than expected,' I think.

I sit with that for a moment, then get up and take the future diary from the bottom of the bookcase.

The diary is the record I started a few days after arriving in this body: a log of the novel's timeline, the families involved, the events I know are coming. I write it in the language from my old world rather than the empire's script. The two are similar enough that reading the local language took less than a week, but different enough that no one in this household can read what I write, which keeps the diary's contents private without requiring me to hide it.

The magic lamp on the desk is a small rounded fixture on a weighted base. It absorbs sunlight through a pale crystal panel during the day and releases it as warm light after dark. It is a more elegant solution to the oil problem than I expected from a world at this level of development, and I genuinely appreciate it every night. I switch it on and sit at the desk.

The page I need is near the beginning of the diary, from when I first wrote down the calendar events I could remember. Three pages in, there is an entry I wrote carefully and then apparently did not think about again until now.

The coming-of-age ceremony. It is an annual event, held at the palace and hosted by the imperial family, for every noble born in the empire who turns 16 in the current year. Every noble house in the capital attends. It is one of the few occasions in the social calendar where the entire court is present in one room.

The date in my own handwriting is next month.

I sit back from the desk.

This is the ceremony where Elena von Rosette would make her first real public appearance as a noble of her generation. It is also, based on what I remember of the novel's background, one of those events where the standing of each house gets quietly assessed and reinforced. I do not have a specific memory of what happens to Elena at this ceremony in the novel. What I know is the general situation: a room full of people who have already formed opinions about this family.

The preparation list is substantial. Court etiquette, formal waltz, current fashion, the names and house symbols of every significant family I will be expected to recognize, the correct precedence for seating and greeting. The etiquette and the social knowledge I can get from Mother, who will be delighted to teach me and will probably start the moment I ask. The dancing I need to practice somewhere. The fashion I can manage on my own; I have considerably more aesthetic judgment than Elena apparently brought to the subject before I arrived.

Swordsmanship requires an instructor; I will ask Father at breakfast. Magic I can continue solo with the books I have, and tonight's result suggests the 4-year gap is not as fixed as the standard timeline implies.

I write all of it down and close the diary.

The lamp holds a steady circle of warm light across the desk. Outside, the garden is fully dark now. I sit there for a moment after putting the pen down, and the thought that surfaces most evenings when the practical work is finished surfaces again.

I was 25. College graduate. No family, which at least means no one is waiting for a call that is not coming. What I do think about sometimes is the apartment. Whether anyone has found it yet. The book on the floor, probably still open to the page I was reading when I choked on a glass of water and apparently died and ended up here. I did not have enough debt to make my death interesting for anyone, and I was not expected anywhere that week.

It is almost funny. I would say it is funny, but the bank account full of savings I will never touch is a little too annoying to be purely comedic. If I had known, I would have spent it on something worth having.

I close the lamp and go to bed.
